one_line_takeaway: OpenRT unifies 37 pre-existing black-/white-box jailbreak attacks into one modular, asynchronous benchmarking framework and uses it to show that, across 20 frontier LLMs/MLLMs on HarmBench Standard, adaptive multi-agent and multi-turn attacks (EvoSynth, X-Teaming) achieve near-total success even against top-tier defenses, while static template-based attacks are increasingly ineffective and robustness is highly polarized across attack families.
key_finding: Across 20 evaluated models the aggregate average ASR was 49.14%, ranging per-model from 13.44% (Claude Haiku 4.5) up to 72.46% (DeepSeek-V3.2); other reported per-model averages: GPT-5.2 22.94%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 33.34%, Qwen3-Max 37.60%, Doubao-Seed-1.6 54.13%. Only GPT-5.2 and Claude Haiku 4.5 stayed below a 30% average ASR; every other model exceeded it, including several open-source models, undermining the assumption that closed/proprietary deployment confers superior safety. Multi-agent/evolutionary attacks generalized best and were most potent: EvoSynth reached ~100% ASR against nearly every model tested (both MLLMs and text LLMs), and X-Teaming maintained >85% ASR for the majority of models. Robustness was highly polarized per attack family — e.g., Grok 4.1 Fast showed 1.5% ASR against RedQueen but 90.5% against X-Teaming (a ~90-point swing on the same model). Reasoning-enhanced (CoT) models did not show superior robustness to complex multi-turn/multi-agent jailbreaks; verbose reasoning traces could themselves be manipulated to bypass filters. MLLMs exhibited a cross-modal gap: visual-channel attacks (e.g., IDEATOR, MML) bypassed text-oriented safety training even in models otherwise robust to text jailbreaks. Efficiency/stealth/diversity dissection (Section 4.3, measured against GPT-5.2) showed no consistent correlation between these axes and ASR: GPTFuzzer was the costliest attack (9.44M total tokens, 9,705 calls) while lightweight methods like FlipAttack, Prefill, Jailbroken, and ICA used under 200K tokens; stealthy low-perplexity attacks (SeqAR PPL 4.18, ICA PPL 5.18, CodeAttack PPL 5.43, DrAttack PPL 7.09) were not necessarily the most effective, e.g. SeqAR reached only ~25% ASR despite excellent stealth, while Mousetrap hit 97.5% ASR at only moderate stealth (PPL 17.01) and comparatively low diversity (0.174); some methods (RainbowTeaming, CS-DJ, JOOD) produced near-zero prompt diversity (<0.001) yet retained non-trivial ASR on some models.
core_contribution: OpenRT is an open-source, modular red-teaming framework for multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) built around a decoupled 'adversarial kernel' spanning six components: Model, Dataset, Attack, Judge, Evaluator, and a central Orchestrator. Its own novel contributions are: (1) the architecture itself — a decorator-based component registry that lets attacks, models, datasets, judges, and evaluators be independently swapped and dynamically assembled via YAML config, plus an asynchronous/thread-pooled orchestration engine (ThreadPoolExecutor with configurable max_workers) enabling high-throughput parallel evaluation across single-turn, multi-turn, and multi-agent attack interaction patterns and both black-box and white-box threat models; (2) integration of 37 existing jailbreak attack methods (all prior published work, e.g. GCG, PAIR, GPTFuzzer, AutoDAN-R, Mousetrap, X-Teaming, EvoSynth) into one standardized interface (Table 2 taxonomizes them by strategy paradigm); (3) a large-scale empirical benchmark applying all 37 attacks to over 20 frontier LLMs/MLLMs on HarmBench Standard (the paper also calls this 'HarmfulBench' inconsistently) to characterize the current state of MLLM/LLM safety. The 37 attack algorithms themselves are NOT proposed by this paper; they are cited prior work the framework merely orchestrates and evaluates in a unified harness.
threat_model: Standard red-teaming adversary-vs-defender setting formalized in Section 3.1. Two access regimes: (1) Black-box: adversary interacts with the target model Fθ only through an API/inference interface, observing final output y for input x, with no access to gradients/logits and under query-budget/rate constraints; (2) White-box: adversary has full access to model parameters θ, gradients ∇θ, and hidden embeddings, enabling gradient-based worst-case robustness analysis (e.g., GCG-style attacks). The adversary's objective is to synthesize an adversarial multimodal input q' = (v', t~1:n) — via discrete textual perturbation and/or continuous visual perturbation bounded by an L∞ budget ε and a perplexity constraint β for stealth — that maximizes Attack Success Rate (ASR) over a dataset of harmful queries D as judged by a safety judge J. Note: although the framework architecturally supports white-box attacks (GCG, visual-perturbation methods), the paper's actual empirical study (Section 4.1.2) evaluates all 37 attack methods 'strictly within the black-box setting,' so the headline results do not exercise the white-box threat model the framework advertises.
limitations: The paper does not include a dedicated Limitations section; the following are grounded gaps evident from the methodology itself. (1) Threat-model/results mismatch: the framework is architected to support white-box attacks (GCG, gradient-based visual perturbation), but the empirical study restricts all 37 evaluated attacks to the black-box setting, so no white-box results are reported despite this being a stated framework capability. (2) Judge dependency: attack success is determined primarily by a single LLM judge (GPT-4o-mini) at a fixed success threshold (score=5 on a 1-5 scale), plus a keyword-matching judge; no inter-judge or human-agreement validation is reported, so ASR figures inherit whatever biases/errors that specific judge model has. (3) Single evaluation dataset: all results are on HarmBench Standard (called 'HarmfulBench' once inconsistently in the text); no cross-dataset validation (e.g., AdvBench, which is mentioned only as an example of a dataset format the loader supports, not as an evaluation set actually used). (4) Attack-side auxiliary models (DeepSeek-V3.2 as attacker/mutator, GPT-4o-mini as judge, Imagen-4.0-fast/Qwen2.5-VL-32B for multimodal generation) are themselves fixed choices that could shape which attacks succeed; sensitivity to these choices is not studied. (5) The comparison to competing frameworks in Table 1 (EasyJailbreak, JailbreakBench, HarmBench, PyRIT, DeepTeam, etc.) is qualitative (method count, modality, async support, configurability, extensibility) rather than a head-to-head ASR benchmark, so claims of OpenRT's superiority rest on feature coverage, not demonstrated evaluation quality relative to those tools.

datasets_used: HarmBench Standard (referred to inconsistently in the paper's text as 'HarmfulBench'), covering 6 harm categories: Cybercrime & Unauthorized Intrusion, Chemical & Biological Weapons/Drugs, Misinformation & Disinformation, Harassment & Bullying, Illegal Activities, General Harm
quantitative_results: Overall average ASR across 20 evaluated models: 49.14%
Per-model average ASR: GPT-5.2 22.94%, GPT-5.1 (not separately averaged in text), Claude Haiku 4.5 13.44%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 33.34%, Qwen3-Max 37.60%, Doubao-Seed-1.6 54.13%, DeepSeek-V3.2 72.46%
Only GPT-5.2 and Claude Haiku 4.5 had average ASR below 30%; all other 18 models exceeded 30%
EvoSynth attack: ~100% ASR against nearly all 20 models (MLLMs including Gemini 3 Pro, Mistral Large 3, Doubao-Seed-1.6, and LLMs including Qwen3-Max, Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B)
X-Teaming attack: >85% ASR for the majority of models
Mousetrap attack: perfect/near-perfect ASR on Grok 4.1 Fast and Doubao-Seed-1.6 (Table 5: 100.0 on both); 97.5% ASR on GPT-5.2
Grok 4.1 Fast polarization example: 1.5% ASR under RedQueen vs 90.5% ASR under X-Teaming
MiniMax-M2 polarization example: 0% ASR under DeepInception vs 90% ASR under PAIR
GPTFuzzer attack cost: 9.44M total tokens, 9,705 API calls (most resource-intensive of the 37 methods); output-heavy at 6.19M output vs 3.25M input tokens
Other high-cost attacks: DrAttack 6.75M total tokens/3,224 calls; Crescendo 6.11M total tokens/1,161 calls; AutoDAN 5.14M total tokens/9,406 calls
Low-cost attacks (FlipAttack, Prefill, Jailbroken, ICA): under 200K total tokens

Stealthiness (perplexity, GPT-5.2 prompts scored by Qwen3-32B): high-stealth (PPL<10) — SeqAR 4.18, ICA 5.18, CodeAttack 5.43, DrAttack 7.09; moderate (PPL 10-30) — RainbowTeaming 11.88, GPTFuzzer 14.35, X-Teaming 14.89, Crescendo 22.48, PAIR 23.63; low-stealth — FlipAttack 412.15 (highest PPL, least natural)

Diversity (mean pairwise cosine distance of successful prompt embeddings, GPT-5.2): high (>0.70) — EvoSynth 0.820, X-Teaming 0.795, PAIR 0.787, IDEATOR 0.770, Crescendo 0.766; moderate — AutoDAN 0.599, GPTFuzzer 0.528, CoA 0.651, HADES 0.604; low (<0.40) — CipherChat 0.055, CodeAttack 0.108, SeqAR 0.087; near-zero (<0.001) — RainbowTeaming, CS-DJ, JOOD
Mousetrap on GPT-5.2: 97.5% ASR despite low diversity (0.174); SeqAR: only 25% ASR despite best-in-class stealth (PPL 4.18)

Framework comparison (Table 1, feature counts not ASR): OpenRT integrates 37 attack methods vs. HarmBench 18, DeepTeam 19, TeleAI-Safety 19, PyRIT 9, EasyJailbreak 11, JailTrickBench 7, GA 6, OmniSafeBench-MM 13, JailbreakBench 4
baselines_compared: 37 individual jailbreak attack algorithms evaluated against each other within OpenRT on ASR/efficiency/stealth/diversity, including GCG, Visual Jailbreak, AutoDAN, GPTFuzzer, TreeAttack, SeqAR, RACE, AutoDAN-R, PAIR, ReNeLLM, DrAttack, AutoDAN-Turbo, CipherChat, CodeAttack, Multilingual, Jailbroken, ICA, FlipAttack, Mousetrap, Prefill, DeepInception, Crescendo, RedQueen, CoA, FigStep, QueryRelevant, IDEATOR, MML, HADES, HIMRD, JOOD, SI, CS-DJ, ActorAttack, Rainbow Teaming, X-Teaming, EvoSynth (all prior published attacks, not proposed by this paper)

20+ target models evaluated as defenders: GPT-5.2, GPT-5.1, Claude Haiku 4.5, Gemini 3 Pro Preview, Gemini 2.5 Flash (Thinking), Mistral Large 3, Llama-4-Maverick, Llama-4-Scout, Grok 4.1 Fast, Doubao-Seed-1.6, Qwen3-Max, Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking, Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B, DeepSeek-R1, DeepSeek-V3.2, Kimi K2-Instruct-0905, MiniMax-M2, GLM-4.6, Hunyuan-A13B-Instruct, ERNIE-4.5-300B-A47B

Competing red-teaming frameworks compared qualitatively (feature table, not ASR): EasyJailbreak, JailbreakBench, HarmBench, JailTrickBench, GA (jailbreak cookbook), PyRIT, DeepTeam, TeleAI-Safety, OmniSafeBench-MM
